title: From Creative Risks to Purpose-Driven Business: Sybil Steele's Journey
guestBio: Sybil Steele is a powerhouse creative entrepreneur who has evolved from filmmaker and global brand consultant to wellness pioneer. She's the Founder of S'eau Prima skincare, Co-Founder of Repegs payment app and Solento Tequila, and a mother raising two young adults.
excerpt: Creative entrepreneur Sybil Steele shares her evolution from filmmaker to wellness pioneer, revealing how motherhood and intuition guided her journey to building multiple purpose-driven businesses. This conversation is packed with wisdom on reinvention, showing up authentically, and creating work that truly matters.
metaDescription: Creative entrepreneur Sybil Steele shares her journey from filmmaker to wellness pioneer, building S'eau Prima, Repegs, and Solento Tequila with purpose.
keyTakeaways: Personal transformation and motherhood can be powerful catalysts for entrepreneurial pivots and finding your true purpose.
Showing up as your whole authentic self in business creates deeper connections and more meaningful work.
Building businesses rooted in wellness and self-care rituals can transform both your life and your customers' experiences.
Community-inspired solutions like local payment apps can address real-world problems while fostering connection.
Balancing multiple ventures requires intentional focus on what truly matters and aligns with your values.
Creative risks become less scary when they're aligned with your deeper sense of purpose and intuition.
The journey of reinvention is ongoing and requires courage to let go of previous identities that no longer serve you.

pullQuotes.text: The creative process isn't just about making something beautiful—it's about showing up as your whole self and doing work that matters.

timestamps.label: Sybil's evolution from filmmaker to entrepreneur
The story behind S'eau Prima skincare
Creating Repegs payment app for community
Solento Tequila and slow sipping philosophy

content:
What happens when you give yourself permission to completely reinvent your career—not just once, but multiple times? Sybil Steele knows firsthand. In this powerful conversation with Aimee Rickabus, Sybil opens up about her remarkable evolution from filmmaker and global brand consultant to wellness pioneer and multi-business founder.

Sybil's journey isn't just about changing careers; it's about the courage to follow your intuition even when it leads you down unexpected paths. As the founder of S'eau Prima skincare, co-founder of Repegs payment app, and co-founder of Solento Tequila, she's built a portfolio of businesses that reflect her deepest values and serve her community in meaningful ways.

The Power of Authentic Reinvention

One of the most striking aspects of Sybil's story is how motherhood became a catalyst for transformation. Rather than viewing her role as a mother of two young adults as something that might limit her professional possibilities, she embraced it as a source of clarity and purpose. This shift in perspective didn't happen overnight—it required her to shed old identities that no longer served her and step into a more authentic version of herself.

The creative process, as Sybil explains, isn't just about making something beautiful or commercially viable. It's about showing up as your whole self and creating work that matters. This philosophy permeates every venture she's launched, from the self-care rituals embedded in S'eau Prima to the community-focused vision behind Repegs.

Building Wellness Into Business

S'eau Prima represents more than just another skincare brand—it's Sybil's response to recognizing that true wellness starts with how we care for ourselves daily. The brand is rooted in self-care rituals that go beyond surface-level beauty to address deeper needs for grounding and presence. This approach reflects Sybil's understanding that modern women need products and practices that honor their complexity and support their well-being holistically.

What makes S'eau Prima particularly compelling is how it emerged from Sybil's personal journey rather than market research or trend analysis. She created what she needed first, then discovered that other women were hungry for the same authentic approach to self-care. This inside-out method of business development is something many entrepreneurs struggle with, but Sybil demonstrates how powerful it can be when you trust your instincts.

Community-Centered Innovation

The story behind Repegs, Sybil's local payment app, illustrates her commitment to addressing real community needs through technology. Rather than creating another generic fintech solution, she identified a specific problem within her community and developed a tool that fosters connection while solving practical challenges. This approach to entrepreneurship—starting with community and working outward—offers a refreshing alternative to the typical startup mentality of scaling fast and asking questions later.

Repegs represents the kind of thoughtful innovation that comes from deeply understanding your environment and the people you serve. It's a reminder that some of the most meaningful business opportunities aren't found in Silicon Valley trend reports but in the everyday experiences of real communities.

Balancing Multiple Ventures

Managing multiple businesses while raising two young adults might seem overwhelming, but Sybil approaches this challenge with intentionality and grace. Her secret isn't perfect time management or superhuman energy—it's clarity about what matters most and the willingness to make decisions based on her values rather than external expectations.

This balance extends to her involvement with Solento Tequila and its slow sipping ethos. Even in the spirits industry, Sybil has found ways to infuse her ventures with meaning and mindfulness. The slow sipping philosophy isn't just a marketing concept—it reflects her broader approach to life and business, emphasizing quality, presence, and intentional enjoyment over rushed consumption.

The Courage to Take Creative Risks

Perhaps the most inspiring aspect of Sybil's journey is how she's redefined what it means to take creative risks. Rather than viewing risk as something to minimize or manage, she's embraced it as an essential part of living authentically. Her willingness to leave behind successful but unfulfilling work to pursue ventures aligned with her values demonstrates the kind of courage that many women in business need to develop.

Creative risks become less frightening when they're rooted in purpose rather than ego or external validation. Sybil's story shows us that the biggest risk isn't failure—it's staying in situations that don't allow you to show up as your whole self or contribute meaningfully to the world around you.

For women navigating their own transitions, whether in business, motherhood, or personal growth, Sybil's journey offers both inspiration and practical wisdom. Her experience reminds us that reinvention isn't a one-time event but an ongoing process of alignment, and that our most powerful work often emerges from our willingness to honor both our evolution and our community's needs.

The path Sybil has carved demonstrates that you don't have to choose between commercial success and meaningful impact. When you build businesses from a place of authenticity and service, you create something that sustains both you and the people you serve—and that's the kind of work that truly matters.
